questions.question: In a senior architecture discussion, how would you describe Next.js in the App Router era?
options: A React framework for full-stack web apps combining UI, server logic, routing, rendering strategies, and deployment optimizations.
A React-only CSS preprocessor with optional server rendering plugins.
A Node.js ORM that happens to include file-based routing.
A replacement for React that does not support Server Components.
questions.explanation: At a senior level, Next.js is best framed as a full-stack React framework with integrated primitives for rendering, routing, caching, server execution, and production deployment concerns.

questions.question: What is the most important conceptual difference between the App Router and the legacy Pages Router?
options: The App Router is built around React Server Components, nested layouts, and segment-level conventions.
The App Router only changes file naming from .js to .tsx.
The App Router removes support for dynamic routes.
The App Router cannot perform static generation.
questions.explanation: The App Router introduces a component-and-segment model with Server Components by default, nested layouts, streaming support, and route-level conventions for loading and errors.

questions.question: Why are components in the App Router server components by default?
options: To reduce client-side JavaScript by default and keep data fetching close to the server.
Because client components are deprecated in React.
Because Next.js cannot serialize props for client rendering.
Because server components are required only for static pages.
questions.explanation: Server Components help improve performance by reducing shipped JS and enabling direct server-side data access for initial render paths.

questions.question: What does adding use client at the top of a file do?
options: It marks the module boundary as a Client Component entry point, allowing hooks and browser APIs there.
It forces all nested imports to run only on the server.
It enables ISR for that route automatically.
It disables tree-shaking for the module.
questions.explanation: use client defines a client boundary. Components in that subtree can use client-only capabilities such as state hooks and browser APIs.

questions.question: What senior-level tradeoff should you evaluate before converting a Server Component to a Client Component?
options: Increased client bundle size, hydration cost, and potentially slower initial load.
Loss of all caching capabilities in Next.js.
Inability to navigate with next/link.
Automatic switch to Edge Runtime.
questions.explanation: Moving logic to the client can increase JS payload and hydration work. Senior decisions balance interactivity needs against performance budgets.

questions.question: How should you fetch backend data for initial render in a Server Component?
options: Fetch directly in the Server Component using async and await.
Always fetch in useEffect and pass data upward.
Only fetch through getServerSideProps in the App Router.
Use localStorage as a first step and sync later.
questions.explanation: In App Router, Server Components can perform async fetching directly, reducing client waterfalls and centralizing initial data dependencies on the server.

questions.question: What does cache: no-store on fetch communicate in Next.js?
options: The request should always be dynamic and bypass cached response reuse.
The response must be permanently cached at build time.
The route can only run in middleware.
The fetch call can run only in the browser.
questions.explanation: cache: no-store indicates dynamic data requirements and disables static caching behavior for that fetch path.

questions.question: What is the purpose of next: { revalidate: 60 } on a fetch request?
options: It enables time-based cache revalidation, allowing data to refresh after the specified interval.
It retries failed requests up to 60 times.
It limits route execution to 60ms.
It forces the route into client-side rendering.
questions.explanation: Revalidation intervals provide a balance between freshness and performance by letting cached content be updated periodically.

questions.question: When should a senior engineer choose fully dynamic rendering over static generation?
options: When output depends on per-request state like auth headers, cookies, or highly volatile personalized data.
Only when the page contains images.
When build times are short.
Whenever route groups are used.
questions.explanation: Dynamic rendering is appropriate when response content truly depends on request-time context or rapidly changing data that cannot be precomputed safely.

questions.question: What is generateStaticParams used for in dynamic App Router segments?
options: Predefining route params at build time for static generation of selected paths.
Declaring runtime middleware conditions.
Registering API route method handlers.
Generating TypeScript types for params automatically.
questions.explanation: generateStaticParams allows known dynamic paths to be pre-rendered, improving performance and reducing runtime work.

questions.question: How does loading.tsx improve perceived performance in nested routes?
options: It provides segment-level loading UI while data or components stream in.
It disables suspense boundaries to avoid flicker.
It precompiles all CSS globally.
It replaces the need for error boundaries.
questions.explanation: loading.tsx acts as a route-segment fallback, enabling progressive rendering and better perceived responsiveness during async work.

questions.question: What is the role of error.tsx in App Router segments?
options: It defines a segment-scoped error boundary UI and recovery flow.
It globally replaces all HTTP error codes.
It validates API inputs before route handlers run.
It configures ESLint severity levels.
questions.explanation: error.tsx handles rendering fallback UI for runtime errors within a segment boundary, isolating failures from the rest of the app.

questions.question: What does not-found.tsx primarily provide?
options: A route segment UI for not found states triggered by notFound() or unmatched paths.
A static file served at /404 only in production.
An API handler for 404 JSON responses.
A middleware-only fallback for missing assets.
questions.explanation: not-found.tsx provides an idiomatic segment-level 404 experience that integrates with App Router control flow.

questions.question: In App Router, what is route.ts typically used for?
options: Defining HTTP method handlers like GET and POST for endpoint logic.
Configuring client-only route transitions.
Declaring image optimization settings globally.
Replacing next.config.ts per segment.
questions.explanation: route.ts allows colocated API endpoint behavior in the App Router by exporting method-specific handlers using the Web Request and Response model.

questions.question: What is a strong reason to choose the Edge Runtime for a route?
options: Lower-latency execution near users for lightweight request processing.
Full Node.js core module compatibility without constraints.
Guaranteed lower cost for every workload type.
Automatic SQL connection pooling with no configuration.
questions.explanation: Edge Runtime is useful for latency-sensitive, lightweight logic distributed geographically, but it has different runtime constraints than Node.js.

questions.question: What is a common limitation when moving Node-centric code to Edge Runtime?
options: Some Node.js APIs and native modules are unavailable or incompatible.
React hooks stop working in client components.
Static generation is disabled globally.
next/link prefetch cannot be used.
questions.explanation: Edge uses a web-standard runtime model, so Node-specific libraries may fail if they depend on unsupported Node APIs.

questions.question: When is middleware the right tool in Next.js architecture?
options: For lightweight request-time concerns such as redirects, rewrites, and coarse auth gating before route handling.
For heavy database joins and report generation.
For rendering server components.
For replacing route handlers entirely.
questions.explanation: Middleware runs early in the request lifecycle and should stay fast and focused on cross-cutting request routing concerns.

questions.question: What is a key anti-pattern for middleware in production systems?
options: Performing heavy, latency-sensitive business logic on every request.
Using redirects for locale detection.
Reading request headers.
Conditionally skipping middleware with matchers.
questions.explanation: Because middleware can run frequently and early, expensive logic there can degrade system-wide latency and scalability.

questions.question: What do Server Actions primarily improve compared to traditional client-submitted API calls?
options: They allow direct server mutations from form and action flows with reduced client boilerplate.
They remove the need for authentication entirely.
They force all pages to be statically generated.
They replace route handlers for read-only requests.
questions.explanation: Server Actions streamline mutation paths by letting forms and components call server-side code directly through framework-managed boundaries.

questions.question: From a security perspective, how should Server Actions be treated?
options: As public server entry points that still require validation, authorization, and safe data handling.
As private-only code that never needs auth checks.
As compile-time utilities with no runtime exposure.
As client-side helpers that cannot mutate server state.
questions.explanation: Even when framework ergonomics are improved, mutation endpoints must still enforce authorization, input validation, and business constraints.

questions.question: What is the purpose of revalidatePath after a successful mutation?
options: To invalidate cached content associated with a specific path so users see fresh data.
To refresh browser localStorage entries only.
To restart the Next.js server process.
To force webpack recompilation in production.
questions.explanation: Path invalidation is a core cache consistency strategy after writes that affect page output.

questions.question: When is revalidateTag preferable to revalidatePath?
options: When multiple pages share data and you want semantic cache invalidation by data domain.
When you need to rotate JWT secrets.
When invalidating only CSS assets.
When route parameters are unknown at build time.
questions.explanation: Tag-based invalidation scales better when many routes depend on shared datasets such as products, posts, or account records.

questions.question: How do fetch cache tags support large applications?
options: They decouple cache invalidation from URL structures by grouping related data under domain-level tags.
They replace database indexes.
They work only in client components.
They disable route segment caching.
questions.explanation: Tags let teams map invalidation to business entities rather than brittle path trees, improving maintainability as systems grow.

questions.question: What is the recommended use of generateMetadata in App Router?
options: Producing route-specific SEO and social metadata, often from server-fetched content.
Declaring middleware matchers.
Configuring transpilation plugins.
Replacing static robots.txt files.
questions.explanation: generateMetadata lets metadata be data-driven per route, which is important for dynamic content SEO and shareability.

questions.question: What is a common senior concern when generating metadata from remote APIs?
options: Controlling latency and failure behavior so metadata generation does not degrade page reliability.
Ensuring metadata runs only in client components.
Avoiding all caching for metadata requests.
Running metadata only in middleware.
questions.explanation: Metadata generation is on the critical path for document output. Engineers should design for timeouts, fallbacks, and consistent caching policy.

questions.question: Why does next/image matter in production beyond convenience?
options: It provides optimized image delivery features such as resizing and modern format handling for better performance.
It is required to use any image in React.
It inlines all images into HTML for speed.
It replaces CDN usage entirely.
questions.explanation: Image optimization reduces transfer size and improves Core Web Vitals when configured correctly, especially at scale.

questions.question: Why must remote image hosts be explicitly configured?
options: To enforce an allowlist and prevent arbitrary remote image optimization abuse.
Because Next.js cannot load local images.
To enable client-side hydration.
Because SVG is unsupported otherwise.
questions.explanation: Explicit remote patterns are a safety and control mechanism for external image sources.

questions.question: What advantage does next/font provide in senior performance tuning?
options: It self-hosts and optimizes fonts to reduce layout shift and external dependency overhead.
It compiles font files into JavaScript bundles only.
It disables browser font fallback.
It is used only for PDF generation.
questions.explanation: Font optimization impacts CLS and first render quality. next/font helps control loading and reduces external request risk.

questions.question: What is the core purpose of next/link prefetching in App Router navigation?
options: To improve transition speed by preparing route assets and data ahead of navigation.
To permanently cache every visited page in the browser.
To bypass middleware for internal links.
To force full page reloads.
questions.explanation: Prefetching can reduce perceived navigation latency, especially for likely next-click routes.

questions.question: What does router.refresh do in App Router clients?
options: It requests a fresh server render for the current route while preserving client-side state where applicable.
It clears browser cookies and session storage.
It rebuilds the app with Turbopack.
It restarts middleware execution in dev tools only.
questions.explanation: router.refresh is useful after mutations when you need updated server-rendered data without a full hard reload.

questions.question: When are parallel routes strategically useful?
options: When you need multiple independently rendered UI slots, such as dashboards with concurrent panels.
Only for static export projects.
When replacing all nested layouts.
Only when using Pages Router fallback mode.
questions.explanation: Parallel routes enable sophisticated compositions where different UI regions can load and update independently.

questions.question: What interview-worthy use case best fits intercepting routes?
options: Opening contextual overlays like login or item detail modals without losing background route context.
Replacing route handlers for webhook verification.
Compiling SVG assets at request time.
Bypassing Suspense boundaries globally.
questions.explanation: Intercepting routes are often used for modal UX patterns where navigation context should be preserved.

questions.question: How do layouts differ from templates in App Router behavior?
options: Layouts persist across navigation in their segment scope, while templates remount and re-run on navigation.
Templates are persistent and layouts always remount.
They are aliases for the same file behavior.
Templates are only valid in client components.
questions.explanation: This distinction is important for state retention and side-effect behavior in complex route trees.

questions.question: Why might a senior engineer intentionally choose template.tsx over layout.tsx?
options: To force remounting behavior for analytics, transitions, or state reset requirements on navigation.
To enable server actions.
To disable route-level error handling.
To replace loading.tsx functionality.
questions.explanation: Templates are useful when per-navigation re-initialization is desired instead of persistent layout state.

questions.question: What is an effective pattern for preventing data-fetch waterfalls in Server Components?
options: Start independent fetches in parallel and await them together.
Nest all fetches sequentially in child components.
Move every fetch into useEffect on the client.
Disable caching globally.
questions.explanation: Parallelizing independent async work reduces total request critical path time and improves TTFB.

questions.question: What is a senior-safe strategy when mixing personalized and cacheable data on one page?
options: Separate personalized dynamic sections from cache-friendly shared content with clear boundaries.
Force the whole page to no-store always.
Render everything in client components to avoid cache rules.
Disable revalidation and rely on manual restarts.
questions.explanation: Good architecture avoids making entire pages dynamic when only a small personalized fragment requires request-time rendering.

questions.question: What is Draft Mode used for in content-heavy Next.js systems?
options: Previewing unpublished or draft content with request-time rendering workflows.
Compiling markdown to static HTML at build only.
Disabling caching for all users permanently.
Switching from App Router to Pages Router during runtime.
questions.explanation: Draft Mode enables secure preview pipelines for editors without exposing unpublished data to all visitors.

questions.question: What is the best high-level auth pattern in modern App Router apps?
options: Use middleware for coarse access control and enforce fine-grained authorization in server logic.
Do all auth checks only in client components.
Store role permissions only in localStorage.
Rely on hidden routes instead of authorization checks.
questions.explanation: Middleware can gate broad access quickly, but authoritative authorization decisions should still happen at the server and business logic boundary.

questions.question: In App Router, where should cookie mutation generally happen?
options: In server contexts such as Server Actions or route handlers where response headers can be controlled.
Inside any random client component render.
Inside next/link prefetch callbacks.
Only during build.
questions.explanation: Cookie writes are an HTTP response concern and should be performed in server-capable execution contexts.

questions.question: Why is environment variable strategy a senior-level Next.js topic?
options: Because separating server-only and client-exposed variables prevents accidental secret leakage.
Because all variables are always safe to expose in client bundles.
Because env vars are ignored when deploying Next.js.
Because only middleware can read env vars.
questions.explanation: Engineers must carefully control what is bundled to clients and what remains server-only to protect credentials and internal endpoints.

questions.question: What is a key production benefit of output: standalone for containerized deployments?
options: It creates a minimal deployable server output with required dependencies for leaner runtime images.
It converts the app to a static-only site.
It disables source maps and logs by default.
It removes the need for Node.js in production.
questions.explanation: Standalone output helps reduce container size and simplify deployment artifacts in CI and cloud environments.

questions.question: How does instrumentation.js help senior teams?
options: It provides a place to initialize observability and runtime instrumentation hooks early in app lifecycle.
It replaces route handlers for telemetry endpoints.
It enables CSS modules globally.
It disables server component rendering overhead.
questions.explanation: Instrumentation entry points support consistent tracing and monitoring setup, which is critical for production diagnostics.

questions.question: What is the main value of route groups like (marketing) and (app)?
options: They organize segments and layout boundaries without affecting URL path structure.
They create private routes inaccessible from browsers.
They force static generation for grouped pages.
They are only valid in middleware folders.
questions.explanation: Route groups improve maintainability of large route trees by separating concerns while preserving URL design.

questions.question: How should a senior engineer decide between Route Handlers and Server Actions for mutations?
options: Use Server Actions for UI-coupled mutations and Route Handlers for API-style integration surfaces.
Always prefer Route Handlers because Server Actions are for reads only.
Always prefer Server Actions even for third-party webhooks.
Avoid both and mutate only in middleware.
questions.explanation: The decision is about interface boundaries: internal UI interactions versus external or API-oriented contracts.

questions.question: What is a robust pattern for webhook endpoints in App Router?
options: Implement them in route handlers with signature verification and idempotency safeguards.
Handle them in client components after hydration.
Process them in loading.tsx.
Use next/link prefetch to trigger webhook processing.
questions.explanation: Webhooks are external server-to-server calls and belong in secure server handlers with verification and retry-safe logic.

questions.question: What does next/dynamic primarily help with in senior frontend performance work?
options: Splitting heavy client-only modules so they load only when needed.
Converting all server components to client components.
Caching API responses on the server.
Generating TypeScript declarations for routes.
questions.explanation: Dynamic imports can defer expensive client bundles, reducing initial payload and improving interaction readiness.

questions.question: Why should server-only code be kept out of client component trees?
options: To avoid bundling sensitive logic or incompatible dependencies into browser code.
Because client components cannot import any modules.
Because Next.js disables TypeScript in server files.
Because server-only code always increases SEO rank.
questions.explanation: Boundary discipline protects secrets, prevents runtime errors, and keeps client bundles clean.

questions.question: How does ISR-style revalidation influence scaling strategy?
options: It reduces full rebuild pressure by refreshing affected content incrementally after deployment.
It disables CDN caching entirely.
It requires every page to be dynamic.
It only works for API routes, not pages.
questions.explanation: Incremental revalidation helps large sites update content without expensive global rebuilds for every data change.

questions.question: What is the goal of on-demand revalidation endpoints in enterprise workflows?
options: Trigger precise cache invalidation from CMS or back-office events when content changes.
Restart the Node process after every content edit.
Disable static rendering in all routes.
Rotate encryption keys hourly.
questions.explanation: On-demand revalidation aligns cache freshness with real content events rather than waiting only for time-based intervals.

questions.question: What is a practical difference between rewrites and redirects in Next.js?
options: Redirects change the visible URL for the client, while rewrites map request handling without changing displayed URL.
Rewrites always return 301 status codes.
Redirects are available only in middleware.
Rewrites can only target static assets.
questions.explanation: Understanding this difference is critical for SEO behavior, analytics interpretation, and migration strategies.

questions.question: When discussing SEO in App Router, what is a key senior-level practice?
options: Ensure metadata strategy, canonical URLs, and dynamic content indexing behavior are explicitly designed per route type.
Rely only on client-side meta tag updates.
Disable server rendering for crawlable pages.
Use one global title for the whole application.
questions.explanation: Serious SEO work in Next.js requires route-aware metadata design and consistent canonicalization decisions.

questions.question: How should Core Web Vitals optimization be approached in mature Next.js apps?
options: Measure real-user metrics and optimize image, font, script, and hydration costs iteratively.
Only optimize Lighthouse scores in local dev once.
Disable streaming to simplify metrics collection.
Move all logic to client components.
questions.explanation: Sustained performance requires telemetry-driven iteration and careful control of render and network costs.

questions.question: What is a strong strategy for handling third-party SDKs in App Router projects?
options: Isolate browser-only SDKs in client boundaries and keep server-side integrations on the server.
Import all SDKs at the root layout regardless of runtime.
Run third-party scripts only in middleware.
Embed all SDK code into route handlers.
questions.explanation: Separating client and server integration paths avoids runtime mismatches and unnecessary client payload growth.

questions.question: Why is idempotency important for server mutations in distributed Next.js deployments?
options: Retries can happen across networks and platforms, so duplicate-safe mutation handling prevents data corruption.
It removes the need for authentication.
It allows skipping database transactions.
It is only relevant for static pages.
questions.explanation: Reliable mutation design accounts for retries and duplicate delivery patterns common in modern distributed systems.

questions.question: What is a practical approach to multi-tenant routing in Next.js?
options: Use host or path-based tenant resolution with clear authorization boundaries and cache partitioning strategy.
Create a separate React app per tenant in one browser tab.
Disable middleware and infer tenant in client localStorage.
Use query strings only and ignore server-side checks.
questions.explanation: Multi-tenant systems require careful request resolution, secure data isolation, and cache correctness across tenants.

questions.question: How should feature flags be integrated in Next.js without harming performance?
options: Evaluate flags close to rendering boundaries and cache appropriately to avoid excessive per-request overhead.
Fetch flags from the client on every component render.
Store all flags directly in global CSS.
Compile a separate framework build for each flag combination only.
questions.explanation: Feature-flag architecture must balance rollout control, latency, and cache behavior across routes and user segments.

questions.question: What does a senior migration strategy from Pages Router to App Router usually prioritize?
options: Incremental route-by-route migration with clear compatibility and observability checkpoints.
A single big-bang rewrite with no dual-routing period.
Deleting all legacy routes before introducing App Router.
Converting everything to client components first.
questions.explanation: Large teams reduce risk by migrating progressively, validating behavior and performance at each stage.

questions.question: How should errors be modeled in production-grade route handlers?
options: With explicit status codes, structured payloads, and logging correlation that supports debugging and incident response.
By always returning 200 with a message field.
By throwing raw stack traces to clients.
By swallowing all exceptions silently.
questions.explanation: Consistent error contracts and observability are essential for reliability and maintainability in API-facing systems.

questions.question: What is the most important reason to define cache policy explicitly in senior Next.js systems?
options: To ensure freshness, correctness, and predictable behavior under scale rather than relying on accidental defaults.
To disable all CDN support.
To avoid using async and await.
To force all components to be client-rendered.
questions.explanation: Explicit cache decisions prevent subtle data staleness bugs and support predictable performance in complex workloads.

questions.question: Which statement best reflects senior-level Next.js interview expectations?
options: You should reason about rendering strategy, cache invalidation, runtime boundaries, security, and operational reliability, not just scaffolding commands.
Memorizing create-next-app prompts is enough for senior roles.
Only CSS and animations matter at senior level.
Senior work in Next.js is mostly about choosing IDE themes.
questions.explanation: Senior interviews emphasize system design judgment across performance, correctness, security, and maintainability in real production scenarios.
